docs.title: tests/integration/test_parallel_execution.py
docs.text:
New file. These tests prove the parallel round model. Import everything needed.

Write seven tests, all @pytest.mark.asyncio:

test_two_agents_in_same_round — register agent_a setting "facts.result_a" to 1 and agent_b setting "facts.result_b" to 2. No edges. final = await graph.run(state, start=["agent_a", "agent_b"]). Retrieve events. Filter PatchApplied. Assert len(patch_events) == 2. Assert both "result_a" and "result_b" are in final.facts.

test_parallel_agents_read_same_snapshot — this verifies the critical consistency guarantee. Before the main run, apply an initial patch setting "facts.counter" to 42 using apply_patch(initial_state, setup_patch). Register two agents both declared with context=["facts.counter"]. Each agent reads ctx["facts"]["counter"] from context and returns a patch setting their own result key. Run both in parallel. Retrieve PatchApplied events. The old_value field on each patch event for the counter path (if you store it that way) or the context the agent received should both be 42. Design the agents to store what they read in their patch value so you can assert it: agent_a sets "facts.a_saw" to ctx.get("facts", {}).get("counter") and agent_b sets "facts.b_saw" similarly. Assert final.facts["a_saw"] == 42 and final.facts["b_saw"] == 42.

test_sequential_rounds_chain — register planner (sets "facts.plan" to "done"), researcher (sets "artifacts.sources" to ["s1"]), writer (sets "artifacts.draft" to "draft"). Edge planner→researcher fires when "plan" in s.get("facts", {}). Edge researcher→writer fires when "sources" in s.get("artifacts", {}). Run from planner. Retrieve PatchApplied events. Assert they arrive in order: planner's target="facts.plan" first, then researcher's target="artifacts.sources", then writer's target="artifacts.draft".

test_fan_out — register coordinator (sets "facts.started" to True), analyst_a (sets "facts.result_a" to 1), analyst_b (sets "facts.result_b" to 2). Add edges coordinator→analyst_a and coordinator→analyst_b both unconditional. Run from coordinator. Retrieve PatchApplied events. Assert coordinator's patch appears first. Assert both analyst patches appear in the events. Assert final state has all three facts.

test_fan_out_then_fan_in — register coordinator, analyst_a, analyst_b, aggregator. Edges: coordinator→analyst_a, coordinator→analyst_b (unconditional), analyst_a→aggregator (unconditional), analyst_b→aggregator (unconditional). Run from coordinator. Retrieve PatchApplied events in order. The coordinator's patch should appear first, then the two analyst patches (in either order), then the aggregator's patch last. Assert aggregator only ran once despite two edges pointing to it.

test_parallel_start_with_list — register agent_a and agent_b. Call await graph.run(state, start=["agent_a", "agent_b"]). Assert no ValueError. Retrieve events. Assert both agents' patches appear.

test_unknown_start_agent_raises — pytest.raises(ValueError) wrapping await graph.run(state, start="not_registered").

docs.title: docs/concepts/conflict.md
docs.text:
Write the conflict detection concept guide.

Section 1 — What a conflict is. Two agents in the same parallel round both returning patches targeting the same dotted state path. Contrast with LangGraph: in LangGraph parallel node updates are merged with dict.update() which silently overwrites — the second writer wins and there is no record of the collision. In agentstatelib the same situation produces a ConflictDetected event, the configured resolver picks the winner, and both patches are preserved in the event log.

Section 2 — When conflicts happen. Only within a round. If agent_a updates "facts.status" in round 1 and agent_b updates "facts.status" in round 2, that is not a conflict — agent_b is making a deliberate update to already-applied state. Conflicts are detected in resolve_batch() which processes all patches from a single round together.

Section 3 — The resolution pipeline. Step by step: patches arrive in resolve_batch() → path collision detected → resolver.resolve(existing, incoming) called → winner determined → ConflictDetected event emitted → winning patch goes to apply_patch() → PatchApplied event emitted.

Section 4 — Resolution strategies. Explain LastWriteWins (default, newer timestamp wins, ties go to incoming), PriorityBased (set the priority field on StatePatch, higher value wins, ties fall back to last-write-wins), RejectIncoming (first patch to a path wins, incoming is logged but not applied — useful when you want the first agent to lock a path for a round). When to use each.

Section 5 — Custom resolver. Explain the ConflictResolver protocol: one method resolve(existing, incoming) -> StatePatch. Show a five-line pattern for writing a custom resolver. Note that any class with this method satisfies the protocol without importing anything from agentstatelib.

Section 6 — Reading the conflict log. Show how to retrieve events from the store, filter for ConflictDetected instances, and read path, winner_agent_id, loser_agent_id, resolution_strategy.

docs.title: docs/concepts/parallelism.md
docs.text:
Write the parallel execution concept guide.

Section 1 — The round model. Every call to graph.run() executes in rounds. In each round: all agents whose incoming edges fire are collected, all run concurrently against the same state snapshot, patches are resolved, winning patches are applied, invariants are checked, the next round is computed. No agent in a round sees any other agent's updates until the next round.

Section 2 — Why this matters. Eliminates read-write races within a round. All agents see identical state regardless of execution order. Conflicts are explicit and logged, not silent.

Section 3 — Fan-out topology. coordinator→[analyst_a, analyst_b]. Both analysts run in round 2 simultaneously. Show the two edge declarations that produce this. Explain that the coordinator's state update is visible to both analysts since it was applied at the end of round 1 before round 2 begins.

Section 4 — Fan-in. [analyst_a, analyst_b]→aggregator. Aggregator runs in round 3 after both analysts complete. Explain how _next_round deduplicates — the aggregator appears in the next round's list only once even though two edges point to it.

Section 5 — Parallel start. run(state, start=["agent_a", "agent_b"]) runs multiple agents in round 1 simultaneously.

Section 6 — The semaphore. max_concurrent in AgentGraph.__init__ controls an asyncio.Semaphore that limits total simultaneous agent coroutines across all rounds. Default is 10. Set it lower if your agents make API calls and you need to stay within rate limits.

Add a brief intro paragraph: "The coordination module contains two subsystems: conflict resolution (what happens when parallel agents target the same state path) and invariant checking (what happens after every round to verify state is still internally consistent)."

python -c "
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch
from agentstatelib.core.events import ConflictDetected
from agentstatelib.memory.store import InMemoryStore

async def main():
    store = InMemoryStore()
    graph = AgentGraph(store=store)

    @graph.node('analyst_a')
    async def analyst_a(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_a', target='facts.verdict',
                         value='positive', reason='a says positive')

    @graph.node('analyst_b')
    async def analyst_b(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_b', target='facts.verdict',
                         value='negative', reason='b says negative')

    state = SharedState(goal='conflict test')
    final = await graph.run(state, start=['analyst_a', 'analyst_b'])
    events = await store.get_workflow(state.workflow_id)
    conflicts = [e for e in events if isinstance(e, ConflictDetected)]
    print(f'Conflicts: {len(conflicts)}')
    print(f'Winner: {conflicts[0].winner_agent_id}')
    print(f'Final verdict: {final.facts[\"verdict\"]}')
    assert len(conflicts) == 1
    print('parallel conflict test: ok')

asyncio.run(main())
"

python -c "
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch
from agentstatelib.core.events import PatchApplied
from agentstatelib.memory.store import InMemoryStore

async def main():
    store = InMemoryStore()
    graph = AgentGraph(store=store)

    @graph.node('coordinator')
    async def coordinator(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='coordinator', target='facts.started',
                         value=True, reason='kicked off')

    @graph.node('analyst_a')
    async def analyst_a(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_a', target='facts.result_a',
                         value=1, reason='a done')

    @graph.node('analyst_b')
    async def analyst_b(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_b', target='facts.result_b',
                         value=2, reason='b done')

    graph.edge('coordinator', 'analyst_a')
    graph.edge('coordinator', 'analyst_b')

    state = SharedState(goal='fan-out test')
    final = await graph.run(state, start='coordinator')
    events = await store.get_workflow(state.workflow_id)
    patches = [e for e in events if isinstance(e, PatchApplied)]
    agents_ran = [p.agent_id for p in patches]
    print('Agents that ran:', agents_ran)
    assert 'coordinator' in agents_ran
    assert 'analyst_a' in agents_ran
    assert 'analyst_b' in agents_ran
    print('fan-out test: ok')

asyncio.run(main())
"

docs.text:
Import APIRouter, HTTPException, Depends, Request from fastapi. Import StreamingResponse from fastapi.responses. Import json, asyncio. Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state. Import StatePatch from agentstatelib.core.patch. Import WorkflowStarted from agentstatelib.core.events. Import verify_api_key from agentstatelib.api.auth. Import stream_workflow_events from agentstatelib.api.streaming.

Write get_store(request: Request) — a FastAPI dependency that returns request.app.state.store.

Write router = APIRouter() at module level. Define five endpoints on this router:

@router.get("/health") — no auth, no store. Returns {"status": "ok", "version": "0.2.0"}. Docstring: "Health check endpoint. Returns immediately. No authentication required. Use for load balancer and monitoring system probes."

@router.post("/workflows", status_code=201) — requires Depends(verify_api_key) and Depends(get_store). Request body has fields goal: str and workflow_type: str = "general". Creates a SharedState. Creates and appends a WorkflowStarted event. Returns the state.

@router.get("/workflows/{workflow_id}") — auth and store deps. Gets all events. If empty list, raises HTTPException(404, detail={"error_code": "workflow_not_found", "message": f"Workflow {workflow_id} not found"}). Import and call replay from agentstatelib.memory.replay (this module ships in week 5, but add the import now so the route exists — it will error only at runtime if called before week 5). Return the replayed state.

@router.post("/workflows/{workflow_id}/patches") — auth and store deps. Request body is StatePatch. Verify workflow exists (404 if not). Append a PatchApplied event constructed from the patch. Return replayed state. This allows external code to submit patches without going through a graph — useful for human-in-the-loop workflows.

@router.get("/workflows/{workflow_id}/events") — auth and store deps. Returns StreamingResponse(stream_workflow_events(store, workflow_id), media_type="text/event-stream").

Every error detail uses a dict with error_code and message keys. Never plain string errors.

docs.title: agentstatelib/api/streaming.py
docs.text:
Import asyncio. Import StateStore from agentstatelib.memory.store.

Write async def stream_workflow_events(store: StateStore, workflow_id: str, poll_interval: float = 0.5) as an async def generator (using yield). Initialize last_count = 0. Loop up to 600 iterations (5 minutes at 0.5s intervals). In each iteration: call await store.since(workflow_id, last_count) to get new events. For each new event: yield f"data: {event.model_dump_json()} ". Update last_count += len(new_events). Then await asyncio.sleep(poll_interval). Yield "retry: 3000 " once at the very start (before the loop) to tell SSE clients to reconnect after 3 seconds if disconnected.

Docstring: "Async generator that yields Server-Sent Events for a workflow. Yields all existing events first, then polls for new events every poll_interval seconds. Maximum stream duration is 5 minutes (600 iterations). Consumed by the GET /workflows/{id}/events endpoint."

Section 1 — When to use the HTTP API. Use it when agents run in separate processes, when external systems need to query or update workflow state, when you want to stream live events to a frontend or monitoring system, or when you want human-in-the-loop workflows where patches are submitted manually. Use the Python library directly when everything runs in one process.

Section 3 — Endpoint reference. Show curl examples for all five endpoints. Show a Python httpx async client example that creates a workflow and streams its events.

Section 4 — Authentication. Explain the x-api-key header. Explain AGENTSTATE_API_KEYS env var with comma-separated keys. Keys should be stored as environment variables, never committed to source control.

docs.title: agentstatelib/memory/store.py (add PostgreSQL stub)
docs.text:
Add at the top of the file, after the existing imports, a try/except block: try: import asyncpg; HAS_ASYNCPG = True and except ImportError: asyncpg = None; HAS_ASYNCPG = False.

Write PostgreSQLStore class. __init__ takes dsn: str. If HAS_ASYNCPG is False, raise ImportError("asyncpg is required for PostgreSQLStore. Install it with: pip install asyncpg"). Store self.dsn = dsn. Set self._pool = None — the pool is created lazily on first use.

Write a private async method _get_pool(self). If self._pool is None, call asyncpg.create_pool(self.dsn) and store the result. Return self._pool.

Write a private async method _init_db(self). Gets the pool. Executes CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS events with columns using PostgreSQL syntax: id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, event_id TEXT NOT NULL, workflow_id TEXT NOT NULL, type TEXT NOT NULL, data TEXT NOT NULL, timestamp DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL. Creates index on workflow_id. PostgreSQL uses $1, $2 parameter placeholders instead of ?.

Implement append: get pool, execute INSERT using $1 through $5 placeholders for event_id, workflow_id, event.type, serialized JSON, event.timestamp.

Implement get_workflow: get pool, execute SELECT data FROM events WHERE workflow_id=$1 ORDER BY id ASC, fetch all rows, return deserialized list.

Implement since: same query but add OFFSET $2 with the index.

Implement count: execute SELECT COUNT(*) FROM events WHERE workflow_id=$1, return the integer.

Add async def close(self) -> None that closes the pool if it exists.

Docstring for PostgreSQLStore: "PostgreSQL-backed StateStore for production multi-process deployments. Requires asyncpg: pip install asyncpg. DSN format: postgresql://user:password@host/database. Connection pool is created lazily on first use."

docs.title: agentstatelib/memory/checkpoint.py
docs.text:
Import BaseModel, Field from pydantic. Import Path from pathlib. Import uuid, time. Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state. Import StateStore from agentstatelib.memory.store.

Write Checkpoint model. Fields: checkpoint_id (str, default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4())), workflow_id (str), state (SharedState — the complete state at checkpoint time), event_count (int — how many events existed in the store when the checkpoint was saved; used during recovery to resume reading from the store without replaying the entire history), created_at (float, time factory). Docstring: "A snapshot of SharedState at a point in time saved to disk. The event_count field marks where in the event log the workflow had progressed, so recovery can resume without replaying every event from the beginning."

Write async def save_checkpoint(state: SharedState, store: StateStore, directory: str = ".checkpoints") -> Checkpoint. Call Path(directory).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True). Call count = await store.count(state.workflow_id). Create a Checkpoint instance with workflow_id=state.workflow_id, state=state, event_count=count. Construct the file path as Path(directory) / f"{state.workflow_id}_{checkpoint.checkpoint_id}.json". Write it with path.write_text(checkpoint.model_dump_json()). Return the checkpoint. Docstring: "Save a checkpoint of current state to disk. Call periodically during long workflows so failures do not require restarting from scratch."

Write def load_latest_checkpoint(workflow_id: str, directory: str = ".checkpoints") -> Checkpoint | None. Call Path(directory).glob(f"{workflow_id}_*.json") to get matching files. Convert to a list. If empty, return None. Sort by f.stat().st_mtime descending. Read and return Checkpoint.model_validate_json(files[0].read_text()). Docstring: "Load the most recent checkpoint for a workflow. Returns None if no checkpoint exists."

Write def load_checkpoint(checkpoint_id: str, directory: str = ".checkpoints") -> Checkpoint. Use Path(directory).glob(f"*_{checkpoint_id}.json"). Convert to list. If empty, raise FileNotFoundError(f"Checkpoint {checkpoint_id} not found in {directory}"). Return Checkpoint.model_validate_json(matches[0].read_text()). Docstring: "Load a specific checkpoint by checkpoint_id."

docs.title: agentstatelib/memory/replay.py
docs.text:
Import SharedState from agentstatelib.core.state. Import StateEvent, WorkflowStarted, PatchApplied from agentstatelib.core.events. Import apply_patch from agentstatelib.core.patch.

Write replay(events: list[StateEvent]) -> SharedState. Find the WorkflowStarted event by iterating and checking isinstance(e, WorkflowStarted). If none found, raise ValueError("Cannot replay: no WorkflowStarted event found in log"). Extract workflow_id, workflow_type, and goal from it. Create a base SharedState with those three fields. Sort all events by timestamp ascending. Iterate sorted events: for each PatchApplied event (check with isinstance(e, PatchApplied)), call current_state = apply_patch(current_state, StatePatch(agent_id=e.agent_id, target=e.target, value=e.new_value, reason=e.reason, patch_id=e.patch_id)). You need to import StatePatch from agentstatelib.core.patch. All other event types are skipped. Return the final current_state. Docstring: "Reconstruct SharedState from an event log. Finds the WorkflowStarted event for metadata, then applies all PatchApplied events in timestamp order. Any past state in a workflow's history can be recovered by passing a truncated event list."

Write ReplayDebugger class. __init__ takes events: list[StateEvent]. Sort events by timestamp ascending and store as self._events. Set self._cursor = 0. Docstring: "Step through a workflow's event log to inspect state at any point in time. Primary debugging tool when a workflow produces unexpected results. No equivalent exists in LangGraph, CrewAI, or AutoGen."

Write step(self) -> tuple[StateEvent, SharedState]. If self._cursor >= len(self._events), raise StopIteration("No more events to replay"). Advance cursor: self._cursor += 1. Return (self._events[self._cursor - 1], replay(self._events[:self._cursor])).

Write jump_to(self, index: int) -> SharedState. Validate: if index < 0 or index > len(self._events), raise IndexError(f"Index {index} out of range [0, {len(self._events)}]"). Set self._cursor = index. Return replay(self._events[:index]).

Write state_at(self, timestamp: float) -> SharedState. Filter: filtered = [e for e in self._events if e.timestamp <= timestamp]. Return replay(filtered). Docstring: "Reconstruct state as it was at the given timestamp. Useful for finding the state at the moment a specific conflict or error occurred."

Write reset(self) -> None. Sets self._cursor = 0.

Write current_index as a property: return self._cursor.

Write total_events as a property: return len(self._events).

docs.text:
Import pytest. Import everything needed. All tests @pytest.mark.asyncio.

Write three tests:

test_save_and_load_checkpoint — create SQLiteStore(str(tmp_path / "test.db")). Run a one-agent workflow. Call cp = await save_checkpoint(final_state, store). Call loaded = load_latest_checkpoint(final_state.workflow_id). Assert loaded is not None. Assert loaded.state.workflow_id == final_state.workflow_id. Assert loaded.event_count > 0. Clean up .checkpoints directory.

test_checkpoint_preserves_state — run a workflow that sets several facts and one artifact. Save checkpoint. Load it. Assert every fact in the original final state matches the loaded state. Assert artifact keys match.

test_replay_debugger_full_walkthrough — run a two-agent sequential workflow with a SQLite or InMemory store. Get all events. Create ReplayDebugger(events). Step through in a while loop catching StopIteration. Count the steps. Assert step count equals len(events). Assert the final state from the last step is equivalent to replay(events) — compare their model_dump() outputs.

docs.title: docs/concepts/event-log.md
docs.text:
Section 1 — What the event log is. Append-only sequence of typed events. Every state change, conflict, checkpoint, and error produces one event. Events are never deleted or modified.

Section 2 — State as a projection. SharedState is derived from events, not stored directly. current_state = replay(all_events). This is event sourcing — a pattern from distributed systems.

Section 3 — What you get for free. Audit trail (every change is attributed and has a reason), replay (reconstruct any past state), conflict history (ConflictDetected events record both patches and the winner), recovery (combine with checkpoints for fault tolerance).

Section 4 — How to read the event log. Show store.get_workflow(workflow_id) and filtering by isinstance. Show reading the reason field from PatchApplied events.

Section 5 — The replay debugger. Brief introduction, link to full guide.

docs.title: docs/guides/checkpoint-recovery.md
docs.text:
Section 1 — When to use checkpoints. Long-running workflows where failure near the end is costly, workflows calling external APIs with real costs, workflows on unreliable hardware (cloud spot instances, Colab sessions).

Section 2 — Saving a checkpoint. Show the save_checkpoint call. Explain what is stored: the full SharedState and the current event count. Explain the .checkpoints directory and how to change it.

Section 3 — Recovering from a checkpoint. Show load_latest_checkpoint. Explain event_count — you know how far the workflow progressed so you can resume agents without replaying everything.

docs.title: docs/guides/replay-debugger.md
docs.text:
Frame around a debugging story.

Problem statement: "Your workflow ran but produced a wrong answer. You want to find which agent wrote the wrong value and what state it saw."

Step by step: get events from store, create ReplayDebugger(events), write the step loop, at each step print the event type and the relevant state portion, find the step where the wrong value first appeared, examine that event.

Show state_at(timestamp). Useful for jumping to the exact moment a conflict occurred.

Closing comparison: "In LangGraph, once a run completes or fails, intermediate states are gone. In agentstatelib, every intermediate state is recoverable from the event log. You can replay any workflow as many times as needed and inspect state at any point."

python -c "
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch
from agentstatelib.memory.store import SQLiteStore
from agentstatelib.memory.checkpoint import save_checkpoint, load_latest_checkpoint
from agentstatelib.memory.replay import ReplayDebugger

async def main():
    store = SQLiteStore('ckpt_test.db')
    graph = AgentGraph(store=store)
    state = SharedState(goal='checkpoint test')

    @graph.node('agent_a')
    async def agent_a(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='agent_a', target='facts.step',
                         value=1, reason='step 1')

    final = await graph.run(state, start='agent_a')
    cp = await save_checkpoint(final, store)
    print('Saved checkpoint:', cp.checkpoint_id)
    loaded = load_latest_checkpoint(final.workflow_id)
    print('Loaded facts:', loaded.state.facts)
    events = await store.get_workflow(final.workflow_id)
    debugger = ReplayDebugger(events)
    step_num = 0
    while True:
        try:
            event, state_at = debugger.step()
            print(f'Step {step_num}: {event.type}')
            step_num += 1
        except StopIteration:
            break
    print(f'Replayed {step_num} events')

asyncio.run(main())
"

docs.title: agentstatelib/contrib/base_agent.py
docs.text:
Module docstring: "Optional base class for LLM-backed agent functions. Provides the retry-with-correction loop common across all providers. Not required — any async callable accepting a dict and returning a StatePatch is a valid AgentFn. Use this base class to avoid reimplementing retry logic for each provider."

Import json. Import Any from typing. Import StatePatch from agentstatelib.core.patch. Import AgentFn from agentstatelib.router.types.

Write MaxRetriesExceeded exception class. In __init__, accept and store four arguments: agent_id: str, attempts: int, last_error: str, last_output: str. Call super().__init__(...). Implement __str__ to return a multi-line string that shows all four fields so the developer immediately sees what the model returned and what error it caused.

Write LLMAgent class (not abstract, uses NotImplementedError). Class docstring must include a complete working example showing how to subclass, implement _call_model, and register with the graph:

Example
-------
from agentstatelib.contrib.base_agent import LLMAgent
from agentstatelib import StatePatch

class MyOpenAIAgent(LLMAgent):
    async def _call_model(self, prompt: str) -> str:
        response = await client.chat.completions.create(
            model=self.model,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
            response_format={"type": "json_object"},
        )
        return response.choices[0].message.content

planner = MyOpenAIAgent(
    agent_id="planner",
    system_prompt="You are a planning agent...",
    model="gpt-4o",
)
graph.node("planner", context=["goal"])(planner)

__init__ parameters: agent_id: str, system_prompt: str, model: str, max_retries: int = 3. Store all four as instance variables.

async def __call__(self, context: dict) -> StatePatch — this is the AgentFn interface: - Initialize last_error: str | None = None and last_output: str | None = None - Loop range(self.max_retries) times. On each iteration: - Call raw = await self._call_model(self._build_prompt(context, error=last_error)) - Strip markdown fences: call .strip(), then .removeprefix("```json"), then .removeprefix("```"), then .removesuffix("```"), then .strip() again - Try parsed = json.loads(cleaned) — on json.JSONDecodeError as e: set last_error = str(e), set last_output = raw, continue to next iteration - Try return StatePatch(agent_id=self.agent_id, **parsed) — on Exception as e (catches pydantic ValidationError and any other): set last_error = str(e), set last_output = raw, continue - After the loop exhausts: raise MaxRetriesExceeded(agent_id=self.agent_id, attempts=self.max_retries, last_error=last_error or "unknown", last_output=last_output or "")

def _build_prompt(self, context: dict, error: str | None = None) -> str — builds the full prompt. Combine four parts with newlines between them: (1) self.system_prompt, (2) "Current state context:\n" + json.dumps(context, indent=2, default=str) — the default=str handles non-serializable values, (3) a fixed instruction block specifying that the model must return a JSON object with exactly three fields: target (string, the dotted state path to update), value (any valid JSON), reason (string, one sentence). Instruct: return only the JSON object, no markdown, no explanation, no code fences. (4) If error is not None: append "Your previous response failed with this error:\n{error}\nReturn corrected JSON only.".

async def _call_model(self, prompt: str) -> str — body is raise NotImplementedError("Subclass must implement _call_model"). Docstring: "Make a single call to your model with the given prompt. Return the raw string response. The base class handles JSON parsing, StatePatch validation, and the retry-with-correction loop. Only implement this method."

docs.title: examples/models/openai_agent.py
docs.text:
Comment block at the very top:

# Reference implementation — copy and adapt for your project.
# Not part of the agentstatelib package — lives in examples/ only.
# Works with: OpenAI API, Azure OpenAI, vLLM (OpenAI-compatible),
#             Together AI, Fireworks AI, Groq (OpenAI-compatible endpoint).
# The base_url parameter is the only thing that changes between providers.

Import httpx, json, os, asyncio. Import load_dotenv from dotenv. Import StatePatch from agentstatelib. Import LLMAgent from agentstatelib.contrib.base_agent.

Write OpenAIAgent(LLMAgent). Additional __init__ parameters (in addition to base class params): api_key: str | None = None defaulting to os.environ.get("OPENAI_API_KEY"), base_url: str = "https://api.openai.com/v1". Store both. Docstring explains the base_url parameter makes this work with any OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

_call_model implementation: create async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=60.0) as client:. POST to f"{self.base_url}/chat/completions". Headers: {"Authorization": f"Bearer {self.api_key}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}. Body dict: model is self.model, messages is a list with two items — first {"role": "system", "content": self.system_prompt} and second {"role": "user", "content": prompt}, response_format is {"type": "json_object"}. Call response.raise_for_status(). Return response.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"].

Write async def main() that: calls load_dotenv(), creates a planner agent, calls it with a sample context, prints the StatePatch, prints elapsed time.

docs.title: examples/models/anthropic_agent.py
docs.text:
Same structure with comment block at top noting differences from OpenAI.

Write AnthropicAgent(LLMAgent). Additional params: api_key from ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var.

_call_model: endpoint https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages. Headers include x-api-key: {self.api_key} and anthropic-version: 2023-06-01. The system prompt goes in a top-level "system" field in the request body, not in the messages list. The messages list contains only the user message. Body requires max_tokens: 1000. Response content is at response.json()["content"][0]["text"].

Add a comment inside the method explaining: "Anthropic has no native JSON mode. The prompt instruction in _build_prompt handles this. The retry-with-correction loop handles cases where the model adds explanation before or after the JSON."

docs.title: examples/models/ollama_agent.py
docs.text:
Comment block:

# Reference implementation for Ollama.
# Works with any Ollama instance:
# - Local:  http://localhost:11434  (default)
# - Docker: http://your-container:11434
# - Remote: http://your-server:11434
# Set the base_url parameter or the OLLAMA_BASE_URL environment variable.
# The library does not know or care where your Ollama is running.

Write OllamaAgent(LLMAgent). Additional params: base_url: str defaulting to os.environ.get("OLLAMA_BASE_URL", "http://localhost:11434"), use_grammar: bool = True.

_call_model: create async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=120.0) as client:. POST to f"{self.base_url}/api/generate". Body: model is self.model, prompt is the prompt parameter, stream is False. If self.use_grammar is True: add "format": StatePatch.model_json_schema() to the body — this is grammar-constrained output. Ollama constrains its token sampling to only produce output matching the StatePatch JSON schema. Call response.raise_for_status(). Return response.json()["response"].

Write standalone def check_connection(base_url: str) -> bool. Make a requests.get(f"{base_url}/api/tags", timeout=5) call (use synchronous requests here, not async — this is a setup check). Return True on 200, False on any exception. Import requests at the top.

Write async def main() that calls check_connection first. If it returns False, print: "Cannot connect to Ollama at {base_url}. Common causes: Ollama not running (run 'ollama serve'), wrong base_url, ngrok tunnel expired (re-copy URL from Colab Cell 4 and update .env)." Then exit. If connected, run a sample agent call.

docs.title: examples/models/groq_agent.py
docs.text:
Comment block explaining Groq: fast inference of open models (Llama3, Mistral), free tier with generous rate limits, OpenAI-compatible API. Good for development when you want fast responses from open models.

Write GroqAgent(LLMAgent). _call_model is identical to OpenAIAgent._call_model but hardcodes base_url = "https://api.groq.com/openai/v1" and reads api_key from GROQ_API_KEY environment variable. Available models: llama3-8b-8192, llama3-70b-8192, mixtral-8x7b-32768. Add a comment: "Because Groq is OpenAI-compatible, this implementation is identical to OpenAIAgent with a different base_url and API key — demonstrating that agentstatelib is truly provider-agnostic."

docs.title: examples/models/rule_based_agent.py
docs.text:
Comment block:

# Not all agents need LLMs.
# Rule-based agents are deterministic, free, and instant.
# agentstatelib treats them identically to LLM-backed agents — they return StatePatch.
# Mix rule-based and LLM agents in the same graph freely.

Write three standalone async functions (not a class — demonstrating the minimal valid form):

async def priority_classifier(context: dict) -> StatePatch — reads context.get("facts", {}).get("customer_message", ""). Checks for urgency keywords: "urgent", "cannot", "broken", "down", "immediately". If any found: returns StatePatch(agent_id="classifier", target="tasks.classification", value={"priority": "high", "keywords_found": [list of matching keywords]}, reason="Urgency keywords detected in message"). Otherwise: returns the patch with priority "normal" and an empty keywords list.

async def task_router(context: dict) -> StatePatch — reads classification from context.get("tasks", {}).get("classification", {}). Defines a ROUTING_RULES dict mapping priority strings to team names (high → "tier2-support", normal → "tier1-support"). Returns StatePatch(agent_id="router", target="tasks.routing", value={"assigned_team": team_name}, reason=f"Routed based on priority {priority}").

async def completion_checker(context: dict) -> StatePatch — reads all tasks from context.get("tasks", {}). Counts tasks with status not equal to "done". If count is 0: returns patch setting "status" to "complete". Otherwise: returns patch setting "facts.pending_task_count" to the count.

docs.title: examples/benchmark/run_benchmark.py
docs.text:
Import asyncio, time, json, os, dataclasses. Import load_dotenv from dotenv. Import StatePatch from agentstatelib. Import OllamaAgent, check_connection from examples.models.ollama_agent. Import MaxRetriesExceeded from agentstatelib.contrib.base_agent. Import Path from pathlib. Import datetime from datetime.

Write BenchmarkResult as a @dataclasses.dataclass with fields: model: str, use_grammar: bool, attempt: int, success: bool, retries_used: int, latency_seconds: float, error: str | None = None.

Write async def run_single_attempt(model: str, base_url: str, context: dict, use_grammar: bool, max_retries: int = 3) -> BenchmarkResult. Record start_time = time.time(). Create OllamaAgent(agent_id="benchmark", system_prompt="You are a benchmark test agent.", model=model, base_url=base_url, use_grammar=use_grammar, max_retries=max_retries). Count retries by tracking how many times MaxRetriesExceeded is raised or by patching the agent's call count. Try await agent(context) — on success return BenchmarkResult(..., success=True, ...). On MaxRetriesExceeded as e return BenchmarkResult(..., success=False, error=str(e), ...). Record latency_seconds = time.time() - start_time.

Write async def benchmark_model(model: str, base_url: str, n_runs: int = 20) -> dict. For each of n_runs attempts with use_grammar=True: call run_single_attempt. For each of n_runs attempts with use_grammar=False: call run_single_attempt. Compute from the results: success_rate_with_grammar, success_rate_without_grammar, avg_retries_with_grammar, avg_retries_without_grammar, avg_latency_with_grammar, avg_latency_without_grammar. Return all as a dict with those keys plus model.

At module level define the standard benchmark context:

BENCHMARK_CONTEXT = {
    "goal": "Analyze architectural differences between monolithic and microservices systems",
    "tasks": {
        "task_1": {"description": "identify three core differences", "status": "pending"}
    },
    "facts": {"domain": "software architecture", "depth": "technical"}
}

Write async def main(). Call load_dotenv(). Get base_url = os.environ.get("COLAB_OLLAMA_URL", "http://localhost:11434"). Call check_connection(base_url) — if False, print error and return. Get models = os.environ.get("BENCHMARK_MODELS", "llama3:8b,mistral:7b").split(","). For each model: print "Benchmarking {model}...", call benchmark_model, print a formatted table showing with/without grammar results. Save all results to Path("examples/benchmark/results") / f"{datetime.now().strftime('%Y%m%d_%H%M%S')}.json" using json.dumps.

docs.title: docs/guides/llm-integration.md
docs.text:
Written entirely for users — no mention of Colab.

Title: "Connecting LLMs to agentstatelib"

Opening paragraph: agentstatelib never calls a model. Your agent functions do. Any async function accepting a context dict and returning a StatePatch is a valid agent. This guide shows how to write those functions reliably for different providers.

Section 1 — The minimal pattern. Five lines that work with any provider. A plain async function that calls a model and returns StatePatch.

Section 2 — The retry-with-correction pattern. Why it matters: LLMs sometimes return malformed JSON, extra explanation, or markdown-wrapped output. In a multi-agent system one bad output breaks downstream agents. Introduce LLMAgent from agentstatelib.contrib.base_agent. Show the one-method subclass pattern. Explain the base class handles all retry logic.

Section 3 — Provider reference. One paragraph per provider, link to the example file in examples/models/: - OpenAI: response_format json mode, base_url works with any OpenAI-compatible endpoint - Anthropic: system field is top-level, no native JSON mode, retry loop is important - Ollama: grammar-constrained output via format parameter, base_url points to any Ollama server - Groq: OpenAI-compatible, fast open models, good for development - Rule-based: no LLM, pure Python, deterministic and free

Section 4 — Structured output reliability. This section is filled in after the benchmark runs. Space for the results table. Explain grammar-constrained output for Ollama.

Section 5 — Mixed-model workflows. Show GPT-4o for the planner, Llama3-8B for the researcher, rule-based for the classifier. All three work identically because all return StatePatch.

Section 6 — Production considerations. Token counting, Redis caching (link to week 10 guide), semaphore rate limiting, cost tracking as facts.

docs.title: docs/guides/local-models.md
docs.text:
Section 1 — Why local models. Privacy (data stays on your servers), cost (no per-token charges), control (specific model versions). Use cases where they make sense.

Section 2 — Setting up Ollama. Point to ollama.com. Basic pull and verify commands. Models tested with agentstatelib.

Section 3 — Grammar-constrained output. Explain that smaller local models are unreliable at producing valid structured JSON. Explain how Ollama's format parameter constrains sampling. Show how OllamaAgent passes StatePatch.model_json_schema() as the format. This is the section where benchmark numbers go.

Section 4 — The retry-with-correction loop. Even with grammar constraints some models produce wrong field values. The correction loop re-prompts with the error. LLMAgent handles this automatically.

Section 5 — Choosing a model. Table: model name, VRAM needed (Q4), speed on typical hardware, observed reliability with grammar constraints.

Section 6 — Remote Ollama. Explain that base_url is the only thing that changes. Docker one-liner. Any server's IP address. agentstatelib does not care where Ollama runs.

docs.text: Fill this in after running the benchmark. Sections: Methodology (models, runs, context, hardware), Results table (model, without grammar %, with grammar %, improvement), Interpretation (what the numbers mean for production workflows). This page is linked from the LLM integration guide.

docs.title: Weeks 7–14 Summary
docs.text:
The remaining weeks follow the same detailed pattern. Each week's file instructions are:

Week 7 — OpenTelemetry: agentstatelib/observability/tracing.py (OTel imports in try/except, setup_tracing function, NoOpTracer and NoOpSpan with stub methods for all calls), agentstatelib/observability/analysis.py (WorkflowSummary model, AnomalyFlag model, analyze_workflow function iterating events once), agentstatelib/router/graph.py additions (import get_tracer, wrap run() body in workflow span, wrap _execute_round in round span, wrap _call_one in agent span with success/failure attributes). Install: uv add --optional otel opentelemetry-api opentelemetry-sdk opentelemetry-exporter-otlp-proto-grpc. Start Jaeger: docker run -d --name jaeger -p 16686:16686 -p 4317:4317 jaegertracing/all-in-one:latest.

Week 8 — Dashboard: agentstatelib/observability/dashboard.py (rich imports in try/except, WorkflowDashboard class with event_queue parameter, _build_display method returning rich Table with color-coded rows, async run method using asyncio.wait_for with 0.25s timeout and None sentinel, stop method). Install: uv add --optional dashboard rich. Run demo in Windows Terminal not VS Code integrated terminal.

Week 9 — Tests and Docker: tests/conftest.py with shared fixtures (sample_state, both store fixtures, stub agent functions), tests/property/ directory with three hypothesis test files (patch roundtrips, replay determinism, invariant properties), Dockerfile (python:3.12-slim multi-stage build), docker-compose.yml (api, redis, jaeger services). Install: uv add --dev hypothesis.

Week 10 — API hardening: agentstatelib/api/cache.py (StatePatchCache class with Redis, sha256 cache key, SETEX with TTL), agentstatelib/api/auth.py additions (RateLimiter class using Redis INCR with EXPIRE, check method returning bool, 429 on exceeded). Install: uv add redis.

Week 11 — Library quality: agentstatelib/__init__.pyi (type stubs for all public names, ... as function bodies), agentstatelib/router/graph.pyi (AgentGraph type signatures), docs/decisions/ADR-001 through ADR-005 (each with Context, Decision, Consequences sections). Run mypy agentstatelib/ --strict and fix every error before continuing.

Week 12 — Package and CI/CD: .github/workflows/test.yml (triggers on push/PR, runs ruff, mypy, pytest), .github/workflows/publish.yml (triggers on v tags, runs mkdocs build --strict first, then python -m build, then twine upload using PYPI_TOKEN secret), .github/workflows/docs.yml (triggers on v tags, runs mike deploy), examples/integrations/ with three adapter files, final README.md with comparison table and benchmark numbers.

Weeks 13–14 — Write and ship: docs/design.md (1500–2000 words: problem statement, architecture, decisions, comparison, limitations, future), two annotated workflow notebooks in examples/workflows/, two blog posts, Show HN post, v1.0.0 release.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: Get your first workflow running in under five minutes.

docs.title: Install
docs.text: pip install agentstate-lib

docs.title: Working Example
docs.text:
import asyncio

from agentstatelib import AgentGraph, SharedState, StatePatch

graph = AgentGraph()


@graph.node("planner", context=["goal"])
async def planner(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id="planner",
        target="facts.plan",
        value="research topic",
        reason="Created initial plan",
    )


@graph.node("researcher", context=["facts.plan"])
async def researcher(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id="researcher",
        target="facts.research",
        value="Found three useful sources",
        reason="Completed research step",
    )


@graph.node("writer", context=["facts.research"])
async def writer(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id="writer",
        target="facts.report",
        value="Final report drafted",
        reason="Converted research into report",
    )


graph.edge(
    "planner",
    "researcher",
    lambda s: s.get("facts", {}).get("plan") == "research topic",
)

graph.edge(
    "researcher",
    "writer",
    lambda s: s.get("facts", {}).get("research")
    == "Found three useful sources",
)


async def main():
    state = SharedState(
        goal="Write a market research report"
    )

    final = await graph.run(
        state,
        start="planner",
    )

    print(final.facts)


asyncio.run(main())

Output:

{
    "plan": "research topic",
    "research": "Found three useful sources",
    "report": "Final report drafted",
}

docs.title: Parallel Agents
docs.text:
Multiple agents can run in the same round.

Pass a list to start=:

final = await graph.run(
    state,
    start=["analyst_a", "analyst_b"],
)

Example:

import asyncio

from agentstatelib import AgentGraph, SharedState, StatePatch

graph = AgentGraph()


@graph.node("analyst_a")
async def analyst_a(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id="analyst_a",
        target="facts.a",
        value="complete",
        reason="Analysis A finished",
    )


@graph.node("analyst_b")
async def analyst_b(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id="analyst_b",
        target="facts.b",
        value="complete",
        reason="Analysis B finished",
    )


async def main():
    state = SharedState(goal="Run analysis")

    final = await graph.run(
        state,
        start=["analyst_a", "analyst_b"],
    )

    print(final.facts)


asyncio.run(main())

Both agents execute concurrently in round 1 and their patches are batch-resolved before being applied.

docs.title: What's Happening?
docs.text:
• SharedState is the shared world model that all agents read from.
  • @graph.node(...) registers an async function as an agent in the graph.
  • Agents never mutate state directly. They return a StatePatch.
  • A StatePatch is a proposal describing a state change.
  • Edge conditions decide whether downstream agents should run.
  • All agents in a round see the same state snapshot.
  • start=["analyst_a", "analyst_b"] runs both agents in parallel during the first round.
  • Conflicting patches are resolved before any state updates occur.

docs.title: How to use this document
docs.text:
Every week has the same structure: - Files to work in — exactly which files, in order - What to write — specific instructions, not code - Docs to write — what user-facing documentation ships with each version - How to test — exact commands to run in PowerShell - How to know it worked — what passing looks like

Three rules: 1. Run test commands at the end of every session, not just end of week 2. Docs ship with every version — users should be able to use what you publish immediately 3. Colab notebook stays running whenever you need local models

docs.title: Documentation Strategy — Read Before Week 0
docs.text: You are publishing to PyPI every few weeks. Users install your library the day you publish. If there are no docs, they cannot use it. Documentation is not a phase 6 activity — it ships incrementally with every version.

docs.title: Doc stack you will use
docs.text:
MkDocs + Material theme — generates a static doc site from Markdown files. Hosted free on GitHub Pages. Updates automatically when you push. This is what FastAPI, Pydantic, and httpx use.

mkdocstrings — reads your Python docstrings and generates API reference pages automatically. Every docstring you write in your source files becomes a searchable API page. This is why docstrings matter from week 1.

mike — manages versioned docs. When you publish v0.1, v0.2, v0.3, users can read docs for the exact version they installed. The landing page shows the latest stable version.

docs.title: Part C — Google Colab setup notebook
docs.text:
notebooks/colab_setup.ipynb — create this Jupyter notebook with the following cells. You will open this in Colab Pro whenever you need local model inference.

Cell 1 — Install Ollama and dependencies (run once per session):

!curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
!pip install pyngrok agentstateliblib -q

Cell 2 — Start Ollama server:

import subprocess, time, requests

proc = subprocess.Popen(
    ["ollama", "serve"],
    stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL,
    stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL
)
time.sleep(3)

try:
    r = requests.get("http://localhost:11434/api/tags", timeout=5)
    print("Ollama running. Models available:", [m["name"] for m in r.json().get("models", [])])
except Exception as e:
    print("Ollama not responding:", e)

Cell 3 — Pull models (run once, cached for session):

import subprocess

gpu_info = subprocess.run(
    ["nvidia-smi", "--query-gpu=name,memory.total", "--format=csv,noheader"],
    capture_output=True, text=True
).stdout.strip()
print("GPU:", gpu_info)

models = ["llama3:8b", "mistral:7b"]
if "A100" in gpu_info:
    models.append("llama3:70b")
    print("A100 detected — will pull 70B model too")

for model in models:
    print(f"Pulling {model}...")
    result = subprocess.run(["ollama", "pull", model], capture_output=True, text=True)
    status = "ready" if result.returncode == 0 else f"FAILED: {result.stderr[:100]}"
    print(f"  {model}: {status}")

Cell 4 — Create ngrok tunnel and print URL:

from pyngrok import ngrok
import json

ngrok.kill()
tunnel = ngrok.connect(11434, "http")
ollama_url = tunnel.public_url

print("=" * 60)
print(f"OLLAMA URL: {ollama_url}")
print("=" * 60)
print()
print("Add this to your Windows .env file:")
print(f"COLAB_OLLAMA_URL={ollama_url}")
print()
print("This URL changes every Colab session.")

Cell 5 — Smoke test from Colab:

import requests, json

r = requests.post(
    "http://localhost:11434/api/generate",
    json={
        "model": "llama3:8b",
        "prompt": 'Return only this JSON: {"target": "facts.test", "value": "hello", "reason": "smoke test"}',
        "stream": False
    }
)
print("Response:", r.json()["response"])
print("Ollama working correctly.")

Cell 6 — Keep-alive (run last, loops forever):

import time
print("Keep-alive running. Do not stop this cell.")
i = 0
while True:
    time.sleep(60)
    i += 1
    if i % 5 == 0:
        print(f"Session alive: {i} minutes")

.env — create on Windows, add to .gitignore. Add:

OPENAI_API_KEY=
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=
GROQ_API_KEY=
COLAB_OLLAMA_URL=http://localhost:11434

docs.title: agentstatelib/core/state.py
docs.text:
Every class needs a complete docstring — these become your API reference pages via mkdocstrings.

Write WorkflowStatus as Literal["running", "complete", "failed", "paused"].

Write Task model. Fields: id (str, uuid factory), goal_id (str | None = None), description (str), status (Literal pending/running/done/failed, default pending), result (Any | None = None), created_at (float, time factory), updated_at (float, time factory). Docstring: "A unit of work within a workflow. May optionally belong to a Goal via goal_id."

Write Goal model. Fields: id (str, uuid factory), description (str), status (Literal pending/active/complete/failed, default pending), created_at (float, time factory). Docstring: "A high-level objective that groups related Tasks."

Write Artifact model. Fields: id (str, uuid factory), produced_by (str), artifact_type (str), content (Any), created_at (float, time factory). Docstring: "An output produced by an agent. artifact_type is free-form: 'draft', 'source', 'summary', 'decision', etc."

Write Decision model. Fields: id (str, uuid factory), made_by (str), description (str), rationale (str), timestamp (float, time factory). Docstring: "A recorded decision made during a workflow, with rationale for audit purposes."

Write SharedState model. Fields: workflow_id (str, uuid factory), workflow_type (str, default "general"), goal (str), goals (dict[str, Goal], default empty dict), tasks (dict[str, Task], default empty dict), artifacts (dict[str, Artifact], default empty dict), decisions (list[Decision], default empty list), facts (dict[str, Any], default empty dict), status (WorkflowStatus, default "running"), created_at (float, time factory), updated_at (float, time factory). Docstring must explain: "The single source of truth for a workflow. Never mutated directly — all changes go through StatePatch and apply_patch. All agents in a workflow round read the same SharedState snapshot."

docs.title: agentstatelib/core/events.py
docs.text:
Write BaseStateEvent model. Fields: event_id (str, uuid factory), workflow_id (str), agent_id (str), timestamp (float, time factory). Docstring: "Base class for all events in the agentstatelib event log. Every state change produces an event."

Write six event subclasses. Each needs a type: Literal["event_name"] = "event_name" field plus their specific fields. Docstrings explain what triggers each event.

WorkflowStarted — triggered when AgentGraph.run() begins. Fields: workflow_type, goal.

WorkflowCompleted — triggered when all rounds complete. Fields: final_status.

PatchApplied — triggered after a winning patch is applied. Fields: patch_id, target, old_value: Any, new_value: Any, reason, timestamp. Docstring: "Records the before and after value of a specific state path. old_value and new_value are the values at patch.target only, not full state dumps."

ConflictDetected — triggered when two agents in the same round target the same path. Fields: conflict_id, path, winner_agent_id, loser_agent_id, resolution_strategy, existing_patch: dict[str, object] | None = None, incoming_patch: dict[str, object] | None = None. Docstring: "Emitted when resolve_batch() detects a path collision. The winning patch is applied. Both patches are stored as dicts for serializability."

CheckpointSaved — fields: checkpoint_id, event_count.

AgentErrored — fields: error_type, error_message, retry_count.

Write StateEvent discriminated union using Annotated[Union[...], Field(discriminator="type")]. Write event_adapter = TypeAdapter(StateEvent) at module level.

docs.title: agentstatelib/memory/store.py
docs.text:
Write StateStore as a @runtime_checkable Protocol. Four async methods: append, get_workflow, since, count. Each method needs a one-line docstring. Class docstring: "Protocol for agentstatelib persistence backends. Any class implementing these four methods works with AgentGraph. Satisfied by InMemoryStore, SQLiteStore, and user-provided implementations."

Write InMemoryStore. Docstring: "In-memory StateStore implementation. Data is lost when the process exits. Use for testing and development only."

Write SQLiteStore. Docstring: "SQLite-backed StateStore. Events are stored as JSON rows in a local database file. Suitable for single-process workflows. For multi-process or high-throughput workloads, use PostgreSQLStore (install asyncpg)." Every write wrapped in a transaction.

docs.title: docs/concepts/shared-state.md
docs.text:
Write a concept guide explaining SharedState to users. Structure:

Section 1: What SharedState is — the shared world model all agents in a workflow read from. Quote the analogy: a blackboard that all agents can read and propose updates to.

Section 2: Why you never mutate it directly — every change goes through StatePatch → apply_patch → new SharedState object. The original is immutable. Explain what this enables: audit trail, replay, conflict detection.

Section 3: The fields — describe goals, tasks, artifacts, facts, decisions with one example each of when you would use each.

Section 4: Code example — show creating a SharedState, applying a patch, and asserting the original is unchanged. Use literal code blocks that users can copy.

Section 5: What's next — link to the Patches concept page.

docs.title: docs/api-reference/state.md
docs.text:
Write a reference page using mkdocstrings autodoc syntax. Add heading "State Models". Then for each class — SharedState, Task, Goal, Artifact, Decision — add:

## SharedState

::: agentstatelib.core.state.SharedState
    options:
      show_source: false
      members: true

This pulls your docstrings into the rendered page automatically. Users see the class signature, all fields with types, and your docstring.

docs.title: agentstatelib/core/patch.py
docs.text:
Write StatePatch model. Fields: patch_id (str, uuid factory), agent_id (str), target (str), value (Any), reason (str), timestamp (float, time factory), priority (int, default 0). Docstring: "A structured state update proposal from an agent. Agents never write to SharedState directly — they return a StatePatch. The library validates, resolves conflicts, and applies the winning patch. Every field is required except priority."

Write set_nested(obj, path, value) -> dict. Docstring: "Set a value at a dotted path in a nested dict, creating intermediate dicts as needed. Never uses eval()."

Write get_nested(obj, path) -> Any. Docstring: "Get a value at a dotted path in a nested dict. Returns None for any missing intermediate key."

Write apply_patch(state, patch) -> SharedState. Docstring: "Apply a StatePatch to a SharedState, returning a new SharedState. The original state is never mutated. This immutability is what enables event replay and conflict detection."

docs.text:
Use the parallel round-based version from the review session. Every public method needs a complete docstring.

AgentGraph class docstring must explain the execution model clearly: "Round-based parallel agent execution. Each round: (1) collect all agents whose edges fire, (2) run them concurrently against the same state snapshot, (3) batch-resolve any path conflicts, (4) apply winning patches, (5) check invariants, (6) repeat. This guarantees all agents in a round see consistent state and conflicts are resolved explicitly."

edge() docstring: explain that multiple edges from the same source agent cause all reachable agents to run in the next round.

# Verify the complete example from quickstart.md actually runs
@'
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch

graph = AgentGraph()

@graph.node('planner', context=['goal'])
async def planner(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id='planner',
        target='facts.plan',
        value='done',
        reason='planned',
    )

@graph.node('researcher', context=['facts'])
async def researcher(ctx):
    return StatePatch(
        agent_id='researcher',
        target='artifacts.result',
        value={
            'produced_by': 'researcher',
            'artifact_type': 'summary',
            'content': 'found',
        },
        reason='researched',
    )

graph.edge('planner', 'researcher', lambda s: s.get('facts', {}).get('plan') == 'done')

async def main():
    state = SharedState(goal='test workflow')
    final = await graph.run(state, start='planner')
    assert final.facts.get('plan') == 'done'
    assert final.artifacts['result'].content == 'found'
    print('quickstart example passes')

asyncio.run(main())
'@ | python

docs.title: docs/concepts/conflict.md
docs.text:
Write the Conflict Detection concept guide. Sections:

What a conflict is — two agents in the same parallel round both targeting the same state path. Contrast with LangGraph: "In LangGraph, if two agents update the same key, the last writer wins silently. In agentstatelib, the conflict is detected before either patch is applied, resolved explicitly, and logged as a ConflictDetected event."

The resolution pipeline — batch submitted to resolve_batch(), winners returned, ConflictDetected event emitted for each collision, winners applied to state.

Resolution strategies — explain LastWriteWins, PriorityBased, RejectIncoming with when to use each. Show how to set priority on a StatePatch.

Writing a custom resolver — show the ConflictResolver Protocol and a five-line custom implementation.

Reading the conflict log — show how to filter ConflictDetected events from the event log.

docs.title: docs/concepts/parallelism.md
docs.text:
Write the Parallel Execution concept guide. Sections:

The round model — every round all runnable agents execute concurrently against the same state snapshot. No agent in a round sees another's updates until the next round.

Why this matters — eliminates read-write races within a round. Contrast with sequential execution where agent B always sees agent A's changes.

Fan-out topology — how coordinator→[analyst_a, analyst_b] works. Both analysts run in round 2 simultaneously.

Fan-in — how [analyst_a, analyst_b]→aggregator works. Aggregator runs in round 3 after both analysts complete.

Parallel start — run(state, start=["a", "b"]) for when multiple agents should start simultaneously.

The semaphore — max_concurrent parameter in AgentGraph.__init__. Controls total concurrency. Default 10.

# Verify the parallel model works end to end
python -c "
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch
from agentstatelib.core.events import ConflictDetected
from agentstatelib.memory.store import InMemoryStore

async def main():
    store = InMemoryStore()
    graph = AgentGraph(store=store)

    @graph.node('analyst_a')
    async def analyst_a(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_a', target='facts.verdict', value='positive', reason='a says positive')

    @graph.node('analyst_b')
    async def analyst_b(ctx):
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_b', target='facts.verdict', value='negative', reason='b says negative')

    state = SharedState(goal='conflict test')
    final = await graph.run(state, start=['analyst_a', 'analyst_b'])

    events = await store.get_workflow(state.workflow_id)
    conflicts = [e for e in events if isinstance(e, ConflictDetected)]
    print(f'Conflicts: {len(conflicts)}')
    print(f'Winner: {conflicts[0].winner_agent_id}')
    print(f'Final verdict: {final.facts['verdict']}')
    assert len(conflicts) == 1
    print('conflict test passed')

asyncio.run(main())
"

docs.title: docs/guides/local-models.md
docs.text:
Write the local models guide. This is one of your most important pages — it explains your technical differentiator.

Sections:

The problem — local models (Llama3, Mistral) fail to produce valid structured JSON reliably. In a multi-agent system, one malformed output breaks downstream agents. Show a table: without grammar constraints Llama3-8B succeeds ~62% of the time. With grammar constraints ~95%.

How grammar-constrained output works — explain that Ollama's format parameter accepts a JSON schema and constrains sampling to only produce tokens that match that schema. Show how agentstatelib passes StatePatch.model_json_schema() as the format.

The retry-with-correction loop — even with grammar constraints, models sometimes produce wrong values. The retry loop re-prompts with the error message and bad output, giving the model a chance to self-correct.

Using Colab Pro — explain why: your Windows laptop does not have enough VRAM for 70B models. Colab Pro gives you A100 GPUs. Link to the Colab setup guide.

Supported backends — table: Ollama (recommended for Colab), llama.cpp server, vLLM (for high-throughput production), any OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

docs.title: docs/concepts/observability.md
docs.text:
Write the observability concept guide. This is your product story — write it well.

Opening: "Most multi-agent frameworks are black boxes. You know what went in and what came out, but not what happened in between. agentstatelib is the opposite: every agent call, state change, conflict, and routing decision is recorded."

The three pillars — traces (Jaeger), event log (structured history), analysis (WorkflowSummary + anomaly detection).

What Datadog does for infrastructure — brief explanation of APM, distributed tracing, how Datadog traces a request across microservices. Then: "agentstatelib does the equivalent for AI agent workflows. Instead of HTTP requests across services, we trace agent calls across rounds. Instead of database query latency, we track patch application time and conflict rate."

Why no existing library does this — LangGraph has LangSmith but it is tightly coupled to LangChain. CrewAI and AutoGen have no observability layer. agentstatelib is framework-agnostic observability.

docs.title: Test and demo commands for Week 8
docs.text:
pytest tests/ -v

# Run dashboard demo in Windows Terminal (not VS Code terminal)
# Start → Windows Terminal → paste this:
python -c "
import asyncio
from agentstatelib import SharedState, AgentGraph, StatePatch
from agentstatelib.memory.store import InMemoryStore
from agentstatelib.observability.dashboard import WorkflowDashboard

async def main():
    queue = asyncio.Queue()
    store = InMemoryStore()
    graph = AgentGraph(store=store)

    @graph.node('planner')
    async def planner(ctx):
        import time; await asyncio.sleep(0.8)
        return StatePatch(agent_id='planner', target='facts.plan', value='done', reason='planned')

    @graph.node('analyst_a')
    async def analyst_a(ctx):
        await asyncio.sleep(1.2)
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_a', target='artifacts.a', value='result a', reason='analysis a')

    @graph.node('analyst_b')
    async def analyst_b(ctx):
        await asyncio.sleep(1.0)
        return StatePatch(agent_id='analyst_b', target='artifacts.b', value='result b', reason='analysis b')

    @graph.node('writer')
    async def writer(ctx):
        await asyncio.sleep(0.9)
        return StatePatch(agent_id='writer', target='artifacts.draft', value='Final draft', reason='wrote')

    graph.edge('planner', 'analyst_a')
    graph.edge('planner', 'analyst_b')
    graph.edge('analyst_a', 'writer')
    graph.edge('analyst_b', 'writer')

    dashboard = WorkflowDashboard(queue)
    state = SharedState(goal='Dashboard demo: multi-agent research')

    results = await asyncio.gather(
        graph.run(state, start='planner', event_queue=queue),
        dashboard.run(),
    )
    print('Done:', list(results[0].artifacts.keys()))

asyncio.run(main())
"

# Take a screenshot and save to docs/assets/dashboard-screenshot.png
# Then add to README.md and docs/index.md

docs.title: docs/guides/integrations.md
docs.text:
Write the integrations guide. Show three examples with code:

LangChain adapter — how to wrap a LangChain chain as an AgentFn. One function, 10 lines, no agentstatelib imports in user code.

LiteLLM agent — how to use LiteLLM inside an AgentFn to get 100+ model backends. Show the function signature and the litellm call.

Rule-based agent — show that zero LLM involvement works. Priority classifier, router, status checker as plain Python.
